text: Judge. Greek, "instruct." (Challoner)<hr>The example of rulers is very powerful. (Worthington)
Therein. Regis ad exemplum totus componitur orbis. (Claud.)<hr>As a whole city is infected with the vices of princes, so it may be corrected by their good behaviour, (continentia.) (Cicero, Leg. 3.)
Rulers. All who wish to live under just kings, Proverbs 29:4.
It, as he did David, Job 34:30., and Daniel 2:20. (Calmet)
The scribe. That is, the man that is wise and learned in the law. (Challoner)<hr>It also denotes an officer, Judges 5:14. God must give light and success. (Calmet)

Injury, out of revenge. (Haydock) (Leviticus 19:18.)<hr>"Caesar used to forget nothing but injuries." (Cicero, Ligar.)<hr>This at least is the character of a great man. (St. Augustine, ep. 138., and 54.)

Men. It invades the rights of the former, and disturbs the repose of mankind.<hr>All. Greek is obscure, "and from both proceeds injustice;" (Calmet) or, "it shall act unjustly." (Haydock)<hr>Pride attacks both God and man. (Grotius)
Deceits. Hence kingdoms are changed. (Worthington)<hr>The ambition and luxury of subjects (Cato) and the negligence of the rulers, bring on confusion. (St. Augustine, City of God 5:13.)
Man. The desire of plunder, or of glory, occasions revolutions. (Haydock)<hr>Covetousness is the root of all evils, and causes people to abandon the faith, 1 Timothy 6:10. (Worthington)
Bowels, and would have no compassion for others or for himself. Some Greek copies leave out as far as sale, but Grabe replaces the omission from the Complutensian.

Beginning, or summit; arche. (Calmet)<hr>Thus Lucifer and Adam fell by pride. (St. Augustine, City of God 12:6.) All sin, being a contempt of God, springs from pride, (Prosper. contemp. 3:3.) and from an inordinate self-love. (Calmet)
Sin, because man abandons God's law, and falls into all misery. (Worthington)<hr>The proud easily yield to all sorts of iniquity. (Menochius)
Disgraced. Greek, "hath taken an exemplary vengeance, and hath," etc. (Haydock)<hr>Them, as he did the giants, Sodom, Nabuchodonosor, etc.
Stead. Luke 1:52. (Calmet)<hr>"Thales being asked what was difficult to be seen; replied, a tyrant grown old." (Laertius 1.)
Nations, the Chanaanites, who submitted to the yoke: or rather the Hebrews. (Calmet)<hr>Greek, "instead of them."
Foundation: overturning Sodom, Babylon, and even Jerusalem, for their sins. (Calmet)

Wise. When Diogenes was exposed to sale, and asked what he could do, he answered, "I know how to command free men." (Laertius 6.)<hr>Joseph and Daniel obtained authority by their wise conduct. (Calmet)
Desert. Let not avarice deprive thee of the necessities of life, nor do any thing beneath thy dignity, Luke 21:19.
Glorified. Greek, "honoured on account of his knowledge, and the rich is, " etc. (Haydock)
And. Greek omits this sentence: (Calmet) yet Grabe has, And the man who is without honour in wealth, how much more so will he be in poverty? (Haydock) (Ecclesiastes 7:12.)
